How to build a profitable ecommerce brand using AI from zero (I tested this myself and got my first sale 1 hour after launching ads):

1. Find a product you're passionate about/know a little something about. Chances are you already know the avatar you're trying to sell to. 

2.
The anti-dropshipping playbook for ecom founders who actually want to scale past $5M: https://t.co/wyOhsElXki

If I had to take a Shopify store from $500K to $2M without increasing your % spent on ads, here's exactly what I'd do:

1. STOP OBSESSING OVER TRAFFIC.

Brands pump money into ads and chase new channels. But if your site doesn't convert, you're pouring more water into a bucket

x.name: oliverb
x.bio: Tabs Chocolate (Exited) || Now building @Bustem__ → we hunt & kill copycats & counterfeits | Book a call below 👇

tweets.text:
U think ur CAC "randomly" doubled overnight.

Nah.

Ur winning creative just got ripped by 15 other brands.

> Same hook
> Same offer
> Same edit style

Now ur competing against urself 5x over.

Welcome to ecom.

Where ur best idea becomes public property in 72 hours.

Snowflakes will hesitate to test a new ad hook for weeks:

> Not be a good look it's borderline schizo
> This is too out there for a small ecom brand
> I'm gonna get doxxed so bad by trolls

Then watch some rando run it on a random Tuesday.

5.4K likes. 1.3M impressions. First

Ur not "burnt out."

Ur just losing.

When revenue's climbing, deals are closing, and the team's shipping?

U don't need coffee to stay motivated, the numbers do that for u.

Winning is the best energy drink.

tweets.text: 100% agree

If a tweet starts with “x just killed y” or any other hyperbole I automatically don’t trust anything that follows. 

I’d say close to 100% of the people making posts like this are either doing it purely as engagement bait or they are being compensated by the company and not

I wish every business owner who spends money on ads had this printed out and hanging above their desk. 

There are so many variables outside of both the advertiser and platforms hands when it comes to performance.

x.name: Kurt Elster
x.bio:
CTO. MBA. The @Shopify expert your developer follows. 
Consultant, host of The Unofficial Shopify Podcast, app dev, & agency owner. Shopify Partner since 2011.

tweets.text:
Did Meta tank or did you?

I met these guys at a conference in Nashville that built a nifty tool that's like a weather report for Meta ads.

> Headwinds pools data across ~50 DTC brands daily so when your numbers crater you can tell whether it's the platform or it's you. https://t.co/I41BmBtYr8

Agentic Storefronts dashboard is live in your Shopify Admin as a Sales Channel.

On a store we work with: ChatGPT is 0.27% of revenue, +42% MoM.

It's a small slice with a steep curve. The right approach here is "monitor monthly" not "overhaul everything." https://t.co/5sqOOf7yhg

Agentic Commerce on a real store:

$39,015 total sales attributed to ChatGPT
or 0.2% of store revenue for the same period.  

Yes, AI makes sales... but it's also still a rounding error. Both can be true. https://t.co/xubN6B0T4r

x.name: Matthew Bertulli
x.bio: Founded 4 companies. 1 great exit -- Co-Host of Operators Podcast -- Just trying to be helpful.

tweets.text:
Are there ANY saas companies who run a really strong performance marketing machine?  

Thinking multi-channel ads here.

Who does this really well?

What he said.

I see too many founders optimizing towards top line growth "SCALE BRO!!" instead of maximizing free cash into their pockets.

Over the last 8 years I've made almost as much money from my investments compounding than I did from my first 2 exits.

If the average person can use AI to get their work done in half the time. 

Do they use the rest to do more work?

Or

Do they just take more time for other things?

Outside of entrepreneurs, I think most people choose to take the free time.
